---
title: "Troubleshooting profile-likelihood CIs: four failure modes"
output:
  rmarkdown::html_vignette:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Troubleshooting profile-likelihood CIs: four failure modes}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment  = "#>",
  eval     = FALSE
)
```

[Profile-likelihood confidence intervals](profile-likelihood-ci.html)
are the package's default for variance components, repeatabilities,
communalities, and phylogenetic signal. They are accurate near
boundaries, respect skewness, and avoid the symmetry assumption of
Wald CIs. **But they can fail.** This article catalogues the four
failure modes that show up in practice and what to do about each.

If a profile call returns `NA` for one or both bounds, an
infinite bound, two bounds that are equal, or a bound that
disagrees sharply with Wald, one of the four cases below applies.

## Failure mode 1: the profile didn't converge

**Symptom**: `tmbprofile_wrapper()` (or any of the derived-quantity
profile extractors) returns `NA` for one or both bounds with no
warning, or emits "profile did not converge" messages.

**Causes**:

- **Boundary parameter (most common)**. A variance component pinned
  near $0$ has no lower-side likelihood ridge to walk along; the
  asymptotic $\chi^2_1$ approximation is one-sided. Lower bound
  comes back as `NA` is correct behaviour; the upper bound should
  still be finite.
- **Joint non-identifiability**. Two parameters are perfectly
  confounded in the data; profiling one leaves the other to drift
  freely. The inner optimisation may diverge or hit the boundary
  on a different parameter.
- **Numerical precision**. A flat profile near the MLE can produce
  spurious `NA`s when the deviance step size is too small to
  resolve the threshold crossing.

**What to do**:

1. Inspect the fit with [`sanity_multi()`](../reference/sanity_multi.html)
   and [`gllvmTMB_diagnose()`](../reference/gllvmTMB_diagnose.html)
   to confirm whether a parameter is boundary-pinned or
   non-identifiable.
2. If boundary-pinned: report a one-sided CI explicitly (cite
   [Self & Liang 1987](https://doi.org/10.2307/2289471) on
   boundary cases).
3. If non-identifiable: simplify the model (drop the redundant
   covariance term) or add a `lambda_constraint` to pin the
   ambiguous direction.
4. As a fallback, switch to `method = "bootstrap"` (parametric
   bootstrap doesn't require the profile to converge, only the
   joint fit to converge on each replicate).

## Failure mode 2: CI bound at $\pm\infty$

**Symptom**: One bound is reported as `Inf` (or `-Inf`); the other
bound is finite.

**Causes**:

- **Effectively no upper bound from the data**. The likelihood is
  monotone in the direction of the boundary: increasing the
  parameter doesn't decrease the likelihood by enough to cross the
  $\chi^2_1$ threshold within any reasonable range.
- **Profile walk hit an internal bound and stopped**. The TMB
  inner optimiser has a soft upper limit on parameter movement per
  step; the profile may terminate early when the walk wanders
  into a region where the inner optim fails to converge.

**What to do**:

1. The `Inf` bound is **honest**: the data don't constrain the
   parameter on that side. Report it as `Inf` in publications;
   don't replace with a Wald upper bound, which would be misleading
   precision.
2. If you need a numerical upper bound for downstream calculations,
   use a parametric bootstrap with a generous `nsim` (say 500 or
   1000); the bootstrap distribution will give you a finite upper
   percentile even when the profile doesn't.
3. Consider whether the parameter is even worth reporting. A
   variance component with $[0.4, \infty)$ tells the reader
   "non-zero, but we have no idea how big". A model simplification
   (drop the component, or pool it with another) may give a more
   defensible inference.

## Failure mode 3: profile flat at the MLE

**Symptom**: `tmbprofile_wrapper()` returns a CI that's much wider
than Wald's, sometimes by an order of magnitude. The
`extract_communality(method = "profile")` output shows
`(lower, upper)` covering almost the full $[0, 1]$ even for a
trait whose communality estimate is unambiguous.

**Causes**:

- **Weak identifiability**. The shared latent structure and the
  unique-variance diagonal are jointly identified only by the
  off-diagonals of the implied $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$; for a trait
  whose off-diagonals are noisy, individual communality is poorly
  pinned. Wald CIs hide this (they read the Hessian at the MLE,
  which may be locally well-conditioned), but the profile sees the
  full likelihood surface and reports the actual flatness.
- **Rotational ambiguity** for individual loadings (not for
  communality, which is rotation-invariant) -- the relevant case
  is when a derived quantity sits at the edge of the rotational
  equivalence class.

**What to do**:

1. The profile is **right** here, not the Wald CI. Trust the wider
   interval and report it as the honest precision.
2. Confirm with [`check_identifiability(fit, sim_reps = 100L)`](../reference/check_identifiability.html):
   if the per-trait Procrustes residual for that loading column is
   large, the corresponding communality estimate is genuinely
   uncertain. Reduce $d$ (the latent rank) and refit.
3. For a sharper CI, get more data (longer time series, more
   sites, more replicates on the noisy trait) or use a model with
   a stronger prior. Profile likelihood cannot manufacture
   information that isn't in the data.

## Failure mode 4: profile and Wald disagree sharply

**Symptom**: $|$Wald lower $-$ profile lower$|$ or
$|$Wald upper $-$ profile upper$|$ exceeds, say, twice the Wald CI's
half-width. The profile is shifted, sometimes substantially, from
the Wald CI.

**Causes**:

- **Non-quadratic profile surface** (most common, and a feature
  not a bug). Variance components, repeatabilities, and any
  parameter near a boundary have non-quadratic profiles by
  construction. Wald assumes the profile is quadratic at the MLE;
  when it isn't, Wald is wrong and profile is right.
- **Skewed sampling distribution**. The same root cause: the MLE
  is far enough from the boundary or the tail that the local
  curvature doesn't match the global shape.
- **Boundary on a different parameter**. If a related parameter
  (e.g. another variance component) is itself boundary-pinned,
  profiling parameter A may force parameter B to the boundary;
  the resulting inner optim behaviour can make the profile look
  jagged.

**What to do**:

1. **Trust the profile**. Wald assumes symmetry the profile does
   not.
2. Plot the profile curve via the underlying
   [`TMB::tmbprofile()`](https://kaskr.github.io/adcomp/_book/Profiling.html)
   call (the wrapper returns the underlying object's
   `parameters`, `value` columns; pass them to `plot()`). A
   visibly skewed or kinked curve confirms the disagreement is
   real, not numerical.
3. For correlations specifically, use
   `extract_correlations(method = "fisher-z")` (the default):
   the Fisher-$z$ transform is the closed-form profile for
   correlations and avoids both the Wald-symmetry assumption and
   the profile-likelihood walk.
4. Consider whether the model is at fault. A near-boundary
   variance component is a sign of over-parameterisation;
   dropping the term and reporting a simpler model may be the
   right answer.

## When to switch to bootstrap

If two or more of the above failure modes hit the same fit (e.g.
boundary-pinned variance + non-quadratic profile + sharp
Wald-profile disagreement), the profile path is fragile. The
parametric bootstrap is the more robust option in these cases:

- `extract_repeatability(method = "bootstrap", nsim = 500)`
- `extract_communality(method = "bootstrap", nsim = 500)`
- `extract_phylo_signal(method = "bootstrap", nsim = 500)`
- `bootstrap_Sigma(fit, n_boot = 500)`

Bootstrap is ~10-30x slower than profile per derived quantity, but
it's a different inference method altogether: it doesn't depend on
the Laplace approximation being a good fit at every grid point of
the profile walk. For a single canonical fit you plan to publish,
500 bootstrap reps takes minutes and gives the gold-standard CI.

## See also

- [Profile-likelihood CIs](profile-likelihood-ci.html) -- the main
  reference for the three-method API.
- [`check_identifiability()`](../reference/check_identifiability.html)
  -- diagnoses weak identifiability via Procrustes-aligned
  simulate-refit.
- [`sanity_multi()`](../reference/sanity_multi.html) and
  [`gllvmTMB_diagnose()`](../reference/gllvmTMB_diagnose.html) --
  generic fit diagnostics that surface boundary-pinned components
  and rotational concerns.
- [Pitfalls](pitfalls.html) -- broader catalogue of model-fitting
  gotchas.
- **Self, S. G. & Liang, K.-Y. (1987)** Asymptotic properties of
  MLE and LRT under nonstandard conditions. *JASA* **82**, 605-610
  -- the canonical reference on boundary cases.
